book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
The Emperor Indignant at the Murder of George, rebukes the Alexandrians by Letter.

ccel.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

title: The Emperor Indignant at the Murder of George, rebukes the Alexandrians by Letter.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The emperor being highly indignant at the assassination of George, wrote to the citizens of Alexandria, rebuking their violence in the strongest terms.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: A report was circulated that those who detested him because of Athanasius, perpetrated this outrage upon George: but as for me I think it is undoubtedly true that such as cherish hostile feelings against particular individuals are often found identified with popular commotions; yet the emperor’s letter evidently attaches the blame to the populace, rather than to any among the Christians.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: George, however, was at that time, and had for some time previously been, exceedingly obnoxious to all classes, which is sufficient to account for the burning indignation of the multitude against him.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: That the emperor charges the people with the crime may be seen from his letter which was expressed in the following terms.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Emperor Cæsar Julian Maximus Augustus to the Citizens of Alexandria.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Even if you have neither respect for Alexander the founder of your city, nor, what is more, for that great and most holy god Serapis; yet how is it you have made no account not only of the universal claims of humanity and social order, but also of what is due to us, to whom all the gods, and especially the mighty Serapis, have assigned the empire of the world, for whose cognizance therefore it became you to reserve all matters of public wrong?

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But perhaps the impulse of rage and indignation, which taking possession of the mind, too often stimulate it to the most atrocious acts, has led you astray.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: It seems, however, that when your fury had in some degree moderated, you aggravated your culpability by adding a most heinous offense to that which had been committed under the excitement of the moment: nor were you, although but the common people, ashamed to perpetrate those very acts on account of which you justly detested them.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: By Serapis I conjure you tell me, for what unjust deed were ye so indignant at George?

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: You will perhaps answer, it was because he exasperated Constantius of blessed memory against you: because he introduced an army into the sacred city: because in consequence the governor of Egypt despoiled the god’s most holy temple of its images, votive offerings, and such other consecrated apparatus as it contained; who, when ye could not endure the sight of such a foul desecration, but attempted to defend the god from sacrilegious hands, or rather to hinder the pillage of what had been consecrated to his service, in contravention of all justice, law, and piety, dared to send armed bands against you.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This he probably did from his dreading George more than Constantius: but he would have consulted better for his own safety had he not been guilty of this tyrannical conduct, but persevered in his former moderation toward you.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Being on all these accounts enraged against George as the adversary of the gods, you have again polluted your sacred city; whereas you ought to have impeached him before the judges.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For had you thus acted, neither murder, nor any other unlawful deed would have been committed; but justice being equitably dispensed, would have preserved you innocent of these disgraceful excesses, while it brought on him the punishment due to his impious crimes.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Thus too, in short, the insolence of those would have been curbed who contemn the gods, and respect neither cities of such magnitude, nor so flourishing a population; but make the barbarities they practice against them the prelude, as it were, of their exercise of power.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Compare therefore this my present letter, with that which I wrote you some time since.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: With what high commendation did I then greet you!

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But now, by the immortal gods, with an equal disposition to praise you I am unable to do so on account of your heinous misdoings.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The people have had the audacity to tear a man in pieces, like dogs; nor have they been subsequently ashamed of this inhuman procedure, nor desirous of purifying their hands from such pollution, that they may stretch them forth in the presence of the gods undefiled by blood.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: You will no doubt be ready to say that George justly merited this chastisement; and we might be disposed perhaps to admit that he deserved still more acute tortures.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Should you farther affirm that on your account he was worthy of these sufferings, even this might also be granted.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But should you add that it became you to inflict the vengeance due to his offenses, that I could by no means acquiesce in; for you have laws to which it is the duty of every one of you to be subject, and to evince your respect for both publicly, as well as in private.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: If any individual should transgress those wise and salutary regulations which were originally constituted for the well-being of the community, does that absolve the rest from obedience to them?

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: It is fortunate for you, ye Alexandrians, that such an atrocity has been perpetrated in our reign, who, by reason of our reverence for the gods, and on account of our grandfather and uncle whose name we bear, and who governed Egypt and your city, still retain a fraternal affection for you.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Assuredly that power which will not suffer itself to be disrespected, and such a government as is possessed of a vigorous and healthy constitution, could not connive at such unbridled licentiousness in its subjects, without unsparingly purging out the dangerous distemper by the application of remedies sufficiently potent.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: We shall however in your case, for the reasons already assigned, restrict ourselves to the more mild and gentle medicine of remonstrance and exhortation; to the which mode of treatment we are persuaded ye will the more readily submit, inasmuch as we understand ye are Greeks by original descent, and also still preserve in your memory and character the traces of the glory of your ancestors.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Let this be published to our citizens of Alexandria.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Such was the emperor’s letter.
